item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The link to the offset is broken. (Forgot www)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Others have said what I want to, but here are a couple questions:

PROTOCOLS FEATURING AGENTS J-eF-EK and K-SAGAN BOTH PROVEN SEMI-SUCCESSFUL: PROTOCOL CONTENT LOST.

I vaguely recollect an SCP written by either Lt_Flops or KindlyTurtleClem entitled "Carl Sagan, Godhead". Is this a reference to that?

We have attempted to shut down the infamous Worm as he did appear, but his winding form appears to have embedded itself most everywhere.

Is this the Destroyer/Threat/WORM, or Anantashesha/twin of the Serpent/enemy of knowledge, or its own thing?

"Effe Tee Elye"

Why is FTL written like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed the build-up, but the keter page has several flaws. At least one of the α events should result in a broken masquerade scenario, and the solution isn't that great or justified either.
The "before" in α-3 should be "after".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Triple pyramoid communication array in Giza Region destroyed beyond repair.

I had no voted until I was driving and thought about this line more. If I'm interpreting it correctly, it's a subtle nod (with huge implications) to the fringe theories that Earth had more advanced civilizations than we believe in the past, but were wiped out in a cataclysmic disaster. And that the great Pyramid wasn't actually built to be a tomb, but was repurposed as a tomb. Meaning that this SCP has visited before, and was the cataclysmic disaster that wiped out the advanced humans of the past. At least that's what I got.

+1 for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'd like this more without the second iteration. For one, I've been seeing an awful lot of those. For two, the first article leaves off with this big "OH SHIT" moment, and sitting down to calmly explain what that moment means followed by some ridiculous plans to stop the big bad was just… not great. +1 for the first half.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there's a before and after here, but the after just falls flat for me. I love the first part, you do a great job of building tension and suspense, the slow realization of oh shit there's something out there and now it's coming for us is really well played.

Unfortunately, the ending is where it goes off, i feel like the whole "lets nuke technology" thing is just a cop-out and a way to deal with things without actually acknowledging the problem. Mixed feelings, but +1 for effort nonetheless, great moxie out there lad!

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

I didn't especially enjoy the buildup of this article. The interview had zero characterisation of Marshall; it seems as if he's more of a character only in there to dumb down the information a little. I think even one or two lines to make him more of a person would help a great deal there.

As for the other parts of the first iteration, I feel there was both too much and not enough worldbuilding. The galactic council meeting was a massive tonal shift for reasons I don't fully understand, being too reminiscent of hitchhiker's guide for a piece like this. While something like it may exist in this world, it's not suitable for a piece like this. Though I'll admit that in a piece where it would fit, it was an entertaining list of things.

For the log after, the static was far too coincidental, only hiding information we'd want in the same way a bad redaction would. It creates mystery artificially instead of the genuine mysteries I enjoy. As for the final iteration, I think nico hit the nail on the head. While the issue is slightly alleviated with the mention that it's a temporary solution, the fact that there's no suggestions of actual solutions and the current one is actively working against any permanent idea means that I still feel very similar. (You've also forgotten to remove the 'permanently' from the conprocs).

As for the collapsible at the end, I don't really get why it's there. I was expecting some form of big stinger or event to subvert what had been set out, but it was an uninteresting empty collapsible. I have some feeling I know what it was meant to represent, but it comes across as too easily mistaken and isn't really as much of a stinger as I was hoping for.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've would have liked to see another answer to Fermi paradox. Civilizations being reaped once they reach a certain point of technological development is the plot of Mass Effect 3, that's predictable.

The idea of uncontacted aliens somehow speaking a language that we can understand perfectly is too much for my suspension of disbelief, even though it's fairly common in this type of story. The exploration of alien ruins also feels under-exploited.

There's one slight change that I really want because I'm a nitpicker and it's my field of study:

Any forms of machine learning systems, artificial intelligence, or otherwise autonomous information compiler are to be destroyed.

If the goal of the Foundation is to bring back humanity to a pre-1980 level of technology, there's no need to get rid of machine learning. It's more about statistics and data analysis than any form of 'learning': there is no form of actual intelligence involved, only mathematics. And it's way older than what you might expect (the most recent method I was taught last semester was invented in 1967). Rather than banning all forms machine learning and models, you should only maintain the first AI winter. Anyway, modern approaches (especially neural networks) would be impossible to create in practice with pre-1980 hardware.

item.title: Re: Space Shenanigans, Meet Death.
item.content:encoded: Huge thanks to my amazing co-conspirator Popsioak as well everyone mentioned above for providing crit along the way. I hope this is as enjoyable to read as it was to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These are all good points. Quite frankly, I didn't consider that. I'll amend the Rudimentum section to mention that it is simply a temporary fix: not a permanent solution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm afraid i don't buy the final conceit of this article. it would cost the foundation countless resources to continuously reset the planet to the 1980's, silence people who would research to advance technology, and hell, wouldn't they eventually become too advanced and end up getting targetted by scp-5000 themselves? this alongside other verisimilitude issues made me come away with the feeling that the foundation depicted in this article is not very bright or incapable of thinking ahead, which is something i very much think the foundation is capable of doing.

item.title: Space Shenanigans, Meet Death.
item.content:encoded:
Like this piece? See it and many more saplings like it, at Popsioak's Garden, here!

Well, it's here. The 5k. With it, a new friend, new opportunities, and new bonds forged.

credits go to: WinterShadow, who helped me through a lot of this piece. Bless him.
Henzoid, Almarduk, and Brewsterion 1017, who provided a lot of early stage critique and help with sorting out what idea I wanted to pursue.
N_Aepic_Fael, JakdragonX, SoullessSingularity, Westrin, Laneous, MalyceGraves, and loads of other people, definitely. I just can't remember them all.

Suggested Accompanied Listening
This Will Destroy You - They Travel On Tracks of Never Ending Light
Beirut - At Once

image creds below :)

+ show block
– hide block
